# Conclusions and Future Work

This thesis examined whether a continuous-control reinforcement learning agent
using order-book-derived microstructure features can achieve economically
meaningful out-of-sample performance in a high-frequency single-asset setting.
Within the controlled experimental design, the answer is affirmative. The TD3
agent outperformed the evaluated benchmark strategies on risk-adjusted return
metrics, and the richer microstructure-aware state representation produced a
clear improvement over the simpler snapshot-based baseline. These findings
support the central claim of the thesis: the informational content of the limit
order book can be translated into state features that materially improve the
behavior of a reinforcement learning trading agent.

The empirical contribution is accompanied by a methodological one. The thesis
developed an end-to-end and reproducible pipeline covering feature engineering
from 10-level order book data, configuration-driven experiment management, TD3
training with a Differential Sharpe Ratio reward, and a strict separation
between training diagnostics and held-out financial evaluation. The results also
show that feature design matters at least as much as algorithm selection in
this setting. The extended feature set yielded more stable positioning, lower
unnecessary turnover, and stronger realized performance than the simpler state
representation, which implies that practitioners and researchers should treat
microstructure-aware feature engineering as a primary design decision rather
than as a secondary refinement.

At the same time, the conclusions must be interpreted within the limits of the
experimental setup. The simulation uses a mid-price execution proxy rather than
executable bid/ask fills, assumes zero market impact and zero latency, and is
evaluated on a narrow three-day AAPL window with a single random seed. These
choices were deliberate because they kept the empirical design tractable and
made it possible to isolate whether the learning problem itself is feasible.
They also place a clear boundary on the claim being made. The thesis
demonstrates feasibility, not deployability. The observed edge cannot yet be
treated as evidence of a production-ready trading strategy, because realistic
execution frictions may materially reduce or eliminate it.

These limitations point directly to the most important implications for future
research and for practical system design. First, transaction-cost sensitivity
must be treated as a binding constraint rather than as a minor robustness check:
any apparent edge that disappears once spread-crossing costs, fees, queue
uncertainty, or latency are introduced should not be interpreted as economically
meaningful. Second, feature engineering deserves priority over marginal changes
in model architecture, because the results suggest that policy quality is driven
primarily by the informational content of the state representation. Third,
stronger claims about generalization require broader temporal and cross-market
evaluation, including longer windows, walk-forward validation, multiple random
seeds, and eventually cross-asset testing.

The most direct directions for future work therefore follow from the current
limitations. The same agents should be re-evaluated under explicit bid/ask
execution rules, calibrated fee schedules, and slippage or market-impact
models. The empirical scope should be extended to longer and more diverse market
periods, including different volatility and liquidity regimes, with multi-seed
reporting to separate genuine policy quality from favorable initialization.
Natural model extensions include richer agent-state variables, broader
cross-asset information, and comparisons with alternative actor-critic or
sequence-model architectures. Taken together, these extensions would test
whether the feasibility result established here survives under more realistic
conditions.

In sum, the thesis shows that combining limit order book data, microstructure-aware
feature engineering, and continuous-action actor-critic learning is a productive
research direction for high-frequency trading. The main result is not that the
deployment problem has been solved, but that the underlying learning problem is
tractable and that the chosen representation carries enough signal to produce a
measurably superior policy in a controlled setting. That is a narrower claim
than a production-performance claim, but it is also the correct one. It
provides a defensible foundation for subsequent work that aims to close the gap
between research-grade feasibility and operational trading realism.
